data_gaps: route_participation and red-zone target share not retrievable (needs nflverse participation/pbp)
injury status unavailable in frozen world
Tyler Warren (rookie): no NFL usage data AND absent from IND depth chart (captured 2025-02-20, pre-draft) -- double gap, confidence capped
Colston Loveland (rookie): no NFL usage data, confidence capped

rankings.rationale: 153 targets at a 25.5% share as a rookie, RISING late (27.2% share, 92.3% snaps, 16.8 PPG) in the pool's second-most pass-leaning offense (62.6%). Target volume this large and this stable is the entire TE thesis; a new QB room changes the thrower, not the funnel.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Target share below 20% across Weeks 1-3 under the new offense.

rankings.rationale: The pool's highest target share (28.6%, 30.7% late) at 89.8% snaps -- and only 2 receiving TDs on 111 catches, an efficiency shortfall that regresses UP toward role average on unchanged volume. Volume plus positive TD regression is the rare double tailwind my rules allow.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Target share below 22% in September.

rankings.rationale: The late window is the signal: 20.8% target share and 89.9% snaps over the last 8 (13.8 PPG), up from a 17.5% season share, in the pool's highest-volume offense. Role ascending, environment elite.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Target share back under 16% under the new coordinator.

rankings.rationale: 94 targets at 20.7% share with 8 TDs -- his 15.8 PPG leaned on TD efficiency I regress, and the late window softened (18.2% share, 13.8 PPG). Samuel's 127 vacated targets are the counterweight that keeps him a clear third.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Target share under 16% early despite the vacated volume.

rankings.rationale: 22.7% target share (24.7% late, 83.9% late snaps) and Cleveland vacated 258 targets -- the pool's largest redistribution -- in its 62.8%-pass offense. The 11-game sample and QB instability are the discounts.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: A target share under 18% in September, or missed time recurring.

rankings.rationale: An 18.6-PPG last 8 on a 22.5% target share is the pool's hottest late window, but at just 64.4% late snaps in an offense whose staff turned over -- the role that produced it isn't structurally guaranteed. Late window weighted, situation-change discount applied.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Snap share above 70% early confirms the role (up 3); a return to rotational usage (down 5+).

rankings.rationale: 133 targets at a 23.3% share is still elite volume even as per-target production fell (12.2 PPG, 823 yards); KC's 163 vacated targets partially reload his funnel. I project the volume with declining-output caution priced in.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Target share below 18% early -- volume, not efficiency, is the sell signal for him.

rankings.rationale: A 92.9% snap share -- the pool's highest -- with an 18.4% target share rising to 20.4% late (12.7 PPG) in a 62-plays/game offense that vacated nothing. Quiet, structural volume.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: A healthy receiver room pushing his share under 14%.

rankings.rationale: The 11 TDs on 69 targets regress hard down, but the late window's 18.6% target share (vs 15.0% season) at least points the right way in a low-volume passing offense (46.3% pass rate) he shares with Likely. Volume-modest, TD-dependent -- ranked on the funnel, not last year's scoreboard.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Likely out-targeting him in September (down 5+).

rankings.rationale: A 25.5% target share in his 9 games -- second in the pool -- now moved to Denver (listed TE1) where 146 targets vacated. The share history says target magnet; the new offense and 9-game sample say caution.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Under 15% target share in his first month in Denver (down 5+).

rankings.rationale: 91 targets at 17.9% share across a full 17 with a strong late window (12.7 PPG) and 118 vacated team targets -- unglamorous, durable volume in a rising offense.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: A drafted/signed TE eating early-down snaps.

rankings.rationale: 17.0% share rising to 19.4% late (11.9 PPG), and Pittsburgh vacated 224 targets with Pickens' exit -- meaningful room for the share to keep growing in a run-lean offense.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: New receivers absorbing the vacated share instead (under 15% for him).

rankings.rationale: 97 targets at a 20.4% share and 80.6% snaps, dead-stable across windows -- high-floor volume in a low-volume offense whose receiver additions may dilute it slightly.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Share under 15% as the new receivers integrate.

rankings.rationale: Rookie with no NFL usage data AND absent from my (February-captured) Indianapolis chart -- a double data gap, so this rank is structural inference: a top pool position implies premium draft capital, and Indy's TE targets were minimal in 2024, leaving the room open. Confidence capped by rule.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Week 1 target share above 15% (up 5+); a blocking-role deployment (down 5+).

rankings.rationale: 19.3% target share when on the field, but 10 games played and a 41.9%-pass offense make both halves of the volume equation fragile. Per-game share earns the rank; availability caps it.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: A healthy 16-game trajectory with share above 18% (up 3-5).

rankings.rationale: An 18.1% target share in his 10-game return at only 61.5% snaps and zero TDs -- a volume base suppressed by injury recovery that a second year removed should lift. The flat late window keeps this a projection of role recovery, not evidence of it.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Snap share above 80% by October confirms recovery (up 5+).

rankings.rationale: 85.8% snaps -- near-Otton security -- but a 15.1% target share in the run-leanest offense in the pool (47.7% pass), with 7 TDs that regress down. Snap floor, target ceiling.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Target share above 19% early (up 5).

rankings.rationale: A 20.2% target share when active against just 57.0% snaps and a TE2 chart listing -- the share says starter, the snaps say rotation, and Buffalo's 99 vacated targets are the upside pathway.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Snap share above 70% early (up 5+); another sub-60% season (down).

rankings.rationale: Rookie, no NFL usage data -- capped by rule. Listed TE1 immediately in an offense that vacated 154 targets (Allen et al.), which is a real structural runway; rookie TEs historically ramp slowly, hence the modest projection.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: A 15%+ target share in September (up 5+).

rankings.rationale: 83 targets at only a 13.2% share and 45.3% snaps -- but in the pool's most pass-heavy offense (63.2%), where even a rotational share produces steady PPR volume.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Snap share collapsing under a new TE rotation.

rankings.rationale: A 17.4% share with ZERO TDs and a fading late window (16.3%, 5.9 PPG) -- but Dallas vacated 135 targets and the TD blank regresses up. Modest bounce-back on volume, not talent claims.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Share under 13% with Prescott healthy (down).

rankings.rationale: Every arrow down: 13.9% season share, 12.9% late, 52.6% late snaps, 5.0 late PPG -- a shrinking role in an offense that vacated nothing to reclaim. Ranked on the trend, not the pedigree.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: A new-QB target spike above 18% share (up 5+).

rankings.rationale: An 11.4% share with a fading late window in the pool's lowest-volume offense -- but Engram's exit vacated 218 targets and he's the listed TE1, so the role could grow under the new staff. Ranked last on evidence, flagged for upside.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: A 16%+ target share in September (up 5+).

rankings.rationale: 13.3% share as the TE2 behind Andrews in a 46.3%-pass offense -- capped twice over, with 6 TDs that regress down. Pure contingency on an Andrews decline or exit.
rankings.what_would_change_my_mind: Out-targeting Andrews in September (up 8+).
